skill.name: ghe-requirements
skill.description: Guides creation, versioning, linking, validation, and backup of GitHub Elements requirement documents.
skill.summary: Manage versioned GitHub Elements requirements and link them to development threads.
skill.author: Emasoft

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly command examples and Markdown patterns, not installed executable code. The real risk is operational: the skill instructs agents to run shell and GitHub CLI commands, edit repository files, and allows writes to hidden Claude configuration, so publication should include a warning.

medium_findings.title: Shell and GitHub CLI Workflows Require Review
medium_findings.description: The static external command findings are true positives in documentation snippets. They run find, sed, cp, git, gh, grep, and awk against repository files and GitHub issues. The intent is requirements management, not malware, but these commands can modify files, commits, and issue comments when executed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly present in shell blocks and have clear side effects. Confidence is not higher because they are examples inside Markdown rather than executable scripts bundled with the skill.

medium_findings.title: Hidden Claude Configuration Write Scope
medium_findings.description: The skill permits background agents to write to ~/.claude for plugin or settings fixes. This is a real filesystem risk because hidden home configuration can affect future agent behavior outside the current project, even though the stated intent is operational support.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permission to write ~/.claude is explicit and semantically relevant to agent configuration. The text limits the purpose, so this is elevated risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Detections Are Markdown Links
low_findings.description: The path traversal findings on requirement links are false positives. The ../ sequences appear inside Markdown links showing how to reference requirement files from issue text, not as filesystem reads or writes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious sequences are visible only in a Markdown example. No code at these lines dereferences the paths or copies data through them.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm detections do not correspond to hashing, encryption, or password handling. They appear to be triggered by Markdown file extensions, requirement examples, or ordinary text in the skill instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic API, digest command, key handling, or credential logic at the flagged locations. The scanner pattern appears overbroad for this Markdown file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repository-Mutating Shell Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Documented commands create files, modify requirement metadata, copy files to backup folders, commit changes, and post GitHub issue content. These operations are expected for the skill but should be executed only in the intended repository.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The side effects are explicit in the command examples. The pattern is dangerous only when an agent executes the snippets without reviewing paths, variables, and repository state.

dangerous_patterns.title: Issue Body Parsing Controls File Validation
dangerous_patterns.description: Validation snippets extract requirement paths and versions from GitHub issue bodies before checking local files. A malformed or unexpected issue body could cause incorrect validation results, although no direct command execution from extracted text was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The issue body is external content that influences path and version checks. Confidence is moderate because the snippet tests file existence and string equality rather than executing extracted paths.

content.value_statement: Feature work often loses context when requirements live only in issue comments. This skill defines versioned requirement files and issue links for GitHub Elements threads.
actual_capabilities: Defines a REQUIREMENTS folder layout with templates, epics, waves, standalone requirements, and a changelog.
Provides a Markdown requirement format with metadata, user stories, acceptance criteria, tests, and revision history.
Describes a semantic versioning workflow for requirement updates and approval commits.
Shows how to link requirement files to GitHub issue threads during development planning.
Includes backup and validation command examples for SERENA memory and linked issue checks.
limitations: It is procedural guidance, not an executable requirements management application.
Shell examples require review before use in a real repository.
It assumes GitHub CLI, Git, Bash, and the GitHub Elements workflow are available.
It does not validate requirement quality beyond the documented checklist structure.

use_cases.title: Create a Requirement Before Development
use_cases.description: Draft a versioned REQ file with user story, acceptance criteria, technical requirements, and planned tests.

use_cases.title: Maintain Requirement Versions
use_cases.description: Update requirement status, version numbers, revision history, and linked issue notices after scope changes.

use_cases.title: Audit Issue Readiness
use_cases.description: Check whether a development issue links to a current requirement file before implementation starts.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Requirement
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a new GHE requirement for this feature request. Include overview, user story, acceptance criteria, technical requirements, tests, and dependencies.

prompt_templates.title: Link a Requirement to an Issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare the GitHub issue requirements section for this REQ file. Include the primary requirement link, version, acceptance criteria, and atomic changes.

prompt_templates.title: Update an Existing Requirement
prompt_templates.prompt: Update this requirement for the new scope. Recommend the semantic version bump, revise affected sections, and describe the linked issue update.

prompt_templates.title: Validate Requirement Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this GHE development issue against its linked requirement. Identify missing links, stale versions, incomplete acceptance criteria, and test coverage gaps.

output_examples.input: A developer asks to start work on a new billing export feature.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates a requirement outline with a REQ identifier, user story, acceptance criteria, functional requirements, atomic changes, tests, and dependencies.

output_examples.input: A requirement changed after a design review.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends a minor version bump, updates the revision history, summarizes the change, and drafts a notice for linked development issues.

output_examples.input: A project coordinator asks whether an issue is ready for development.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks for a requirement link, confirms version alignment, and lists missing acceptance criteria or test requirements.

best_practices: Review every shell command before execution, especially commands that commit, copy, or post issue comments.
Keep requirement files and GitHub issue links synchronized after every scope or version change.
Use clear acceptance criteria and test requirements before development work begins.
anti_patterns: Do not start development from an issue that has no linked requirement file.
Do not silently change user specifications while converting them into requirement documents.
Do not run the documented commands in a repository that does not follow the expected folder layout.

faq.question: What does this skill help manage?
faq.answer: It helps manage GitHub Elements requirement documents, issue links, version updates, backups, and readiness checks.

faq.question: Does it create files automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides procedures and command examples that an agent or user may choose to run.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes shell commands and permits hidden configuration writes. These are useful but require careful review before execution.

faq.question: Can it work outside GitHub Elements?
faq.answer: It can inspire similar workflows, but its paths, labels, and roles are written for GitHub Elements.

faq.question: Does it replace product requirements review?
faq.answer: No. It structures requirements, but humans still need to approve scope, quality, and priority.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
